dial.title: The 24-Hour Dial
dial.base:
[[On the Dial]] Every clock you have ever read divides the day in two and makes you supply the missing half — is it eight in the morning or eight at night? This dial refuses the ambiguity. It carries all twenty-four hours on one face, so the single stroke that marks the time can only mean one thing. Noon sits at the very top, midnight at the very bottom, and the hours run clockwise down the right side through the afternoon and up the left through the small hours — 18h due right, 06h due left, the whole day laid out the way the sun actually walks it.

[[The Hour]] Because a full turn now takes a full day, the hands change roles. The hour hand sweeps the entire circle just once from midnight to midnight, moving at a patient fifteen degrees an hour — slow enough that its angle alone tells you where you are in the day at a glance, high and bright near the top at noon, buried at the bottom at three in the morning. The minute hand keeps its ordinary pace, one turn an hour. There is deliberately no seconds hand: this is a clock for reading the shape of a day, not for timing a race.

[[On the Dial]] The rim is machined like a real instrument. Around the circle run exactly 360 marks — one for every degree, because the whole twenty-four-hour turn is 360°, which makes each degree worth precisely four minutes of time. Twelve white glow-arrows fall on the odd hours, and a thirteenth crowns 12h — noon's own arrow, the brightest of all, standing directly under the gold tip of the star. Eleven short gray ticks take the remaining even hours, midnight quietly among them. The hours filled, the minute divisions fill the gaps: forty-eight medium marks at the thirds of each hour and two hundred eighty-eight fine ones between them. Thirteen and eleven make the twenty-four hours; forty-eight and two hundred eighty-eight make the three hundred thirty-six minute marks; all of it sums to 360, one clean tooth per degree.

[[The Lesson]] The reward for the strangeness is a clock you read like a sundial made honest. When the hour hand stands straight up, the sun is at its height; when it hangs straight down, you are at the dead middle of the night. The colored arc, the twilight bands, the weekday star and the season marker all hang on this one frame — twenty-four hours, top to bottom, the day drawn as it is lived.

solar_rotation.title: Solar Rotation and the Hexagram
solar_rotation.base:
[[The Sky]] The six-pointed star at the center is not decoration and it does not sit still. Its top vertex is a pointer, and what it points at is true solar noon — the actual instant the sun crosses the meridian and stands at its highest, which is almost never the 12:00 your phone shows. So on most days the star is tipped a few degrees off vertical, and that tilt is a real measurement, not a wobble.

[[On the Dial]] Clock noon and sun noon disagree for three honest reasons, and the star sums all three. You usually live east or west of the exact line your time zone is named for, which slides your solar noon earlier or later. The Earth's tilted, elliptical orbit runs the sun a little fast or slow against the clock through the year — the equation of time, worth up to a quarter hour either way. And half the year daylight saving shoves the whole civil clock an hour off the sun. The star takes the day's true noon, measured in seconds since local midnight, and rotates by (noon_secs − 43200) / 240 degrees — a positive, clockwise turn whenever solar noon falls after 12:00 (deep in the western edge of a zone, or under summer time).

[[The Lesson]] Everything mounted on the star turns with it. The weekday bodies ride in diamond slots that rotate as one rigid piece, so the day's planet is always correctly placed relative to the real sun, not the bureaucratic one. The top vertex therefore does double duty: it is the noon pointer and it is the anchor from which the whole symbolic wheel hangs. Look at the star's lean and you are reading, directly off the glass, how far your civil clock has drifted from the sky.

twilight.title: Twilight
twilight.base:
[[The Sky]] Day does not switch to night; it dissolves into it. After the sun's disc drops below the horizon the sky stays lit for a while by sunlight bent and scattered over the edge of the world, and that lingering glow is twilight. Astronomers cut it into three bands by how far the sun has sunk. Civil twilight runs from the horizon to 6° below it — bright enough to walk, read a little, see the brightest stars. Nautical twilight reaches to 12°, the old depth at which a sailor could still fix both the horizon line and the stars for a sextant sight. Astronomical twilight ends at 18°, past which the last faint wash of sun is gone and the sky is truly dark. DOMY draws the civil band — the one a person actually notices, the pale margin before sunrise and after sunset when there is light but no sun.

[[On the Dial]] Those 6° take longer than you would guess. If the sun fell straight down it would cross 6° of altitude in about twenty-four minutes, but it almost never falls straight down. Away from the equator the sun sets at a slant, sidling toward the horizon rather than dropping onto it, so it spends far longer in each degree of depression — at mid-latitudes civil twilight lasts roughly 37 minutes. On this dial, which turns a steady fifteen degrees per hour, 37 minutes of time is 9.25° of arc, a band you can see with your eye. Push the clock toward the poles and the slant grows shallower still: the band stretches to an hour, then hours, and in high summer the sun may never sink the full six degrees at all — which is exactly why the dial's twilight arcs swell so dramatically when you move it north to Tromsø.

[[The Sky]] The colors are physics, not mood-painting. Sunlight scatters off the air more strongly the shorter its wavelength — blue far more than red, the Rayleigh law that makes the daytime sky blue in the first place. At twilight the sun's direct light must graze the whole thick edge of the atmosphere, an enormously long path that scatters the blue clean out and lets only the reddened remainder through — the same reddening that turns the low sun itself orange and, laid over haze and dust, browns the western sky at dusk. The morning's cool blue has a second, subtler cause: high in the atmosphere ozone absorbs in the yellow-orange (its Chappuis band), so the deep-twilight light that has travelled farthest through it comes back tinted blue — the painters' and photographers' "blue hour."

[[The Lesson]] Both ends of the day own both effects, but the dial gives each its signature: the dawn band wears blue on the left, at the 06h sunrise arm, and the dusk band brown on the right, at the 18h sunset. It is honest to the light — a waking sky leads with the blue hour, an ending one leads with the red — and it rhymes with the clock's whole emotional canon, where the pre-dawn arm is Calm and Hope and the sunset arm is Passion and Longing.

year_wheel.title: The Year Wheel
year_wheel.base:
[[The Sky]] Behind the day sits a second, far slower clock: a marker that creeps once around the same face in a year, standing at the top at midsummer and at the bottom in the dark of the December solstice. Its four cardinal points are the hinges of the year — the two solstices top and bottom, the two equinoxes left and right — and they are not approximated. Each is placed on the real astronomical instant, read from the bundled ephemeris of season crossings, so the equinox marker lights up on the exact day the sun crosses the equator.

[[On the Dial]] Yet every season is drawn as an equal quarter. Real seasons are unequal — northern summer runs several days longer than winter because the Earth moves slower when it is farther from the sun — but on this wheel each of the six anchor-to-anchor spans is stretched or squeezed to fill exactly 90°, so the equinoxes land precisely at 90° and 270° and the solstices exactly at top and bottom. Between any two anchors the marker moves at a constant rate (a straight, piecewise-linear interpolation); it simply runs a hair faster through the longer seasons and slower through the shorter ones to keep the corners square.

[[The Lesson]] The choice is the owner's, and it is a legibility choice: a viewer should be able to read "we are a third of the way through spring" off the angle without a calendar, and equal quarters make that possible while the corners stay astronomically true. The tropical zodiac rides this same wheel — twelve 30° signs anchored on the real season instants, Cancer opening at the summer solstice at the very top, Capricorn at the winter solstice below — so the ring of signs inherits the wheel's honest hinges and its tidy geometry at once.

moon_lunations.title: The Moon Wheel and the Lunations
moon_lunations.base:
[[On the Dial]] A third marker keeps the moon. It rides the year wheel at the moon's own position and carries the current phase — new, waxing, full, waning — with a soft glow at each of the four principal phases as they fall. The moon is the fastest of the dial's slow hands, closing its cycle of phases every 29.53 days, and its light on the face is always the sun's light handed on: the wheel shows you, at a glance, where in its month the moon stands and how much of the night it will own.

[[The Sky]] DOMY also numbers the moons of the year, and the rule is exact. The year's first Moon is the first New Moon that falls on or after January 1; the second is the next, and so on. The days at the very start of January, before that first new moon, do not belong to Moon One — they belong to the previous year's last lunation, its twelfth or thirteenth, still running out its cycle across the New Year. A lunation, in other words, is counted by the year its new moon opens in, not by the calendar days it happens to touch.

[[The Lesson]] Which is why a year has twelve moons or thirteen. Twelve lunar months run about 354 days — some eleven days short of the solar year — so the new moons drift steadily earlier each year until, roughly once in three years, a thirteenth new moon squeezes in before December is out. Most years therefore close on their twelfth Moon; about one in three reaches a thirteenth (the "blue moon" years of folklore). The dial simply follows the sky: it starts counting at the first new moon of January and lets the arithmetic of an eleven-day shortfall decide whether the tally ends at twelve or thirteen.

paint_light.title: Paint and Light
paint_light.base:
[[The Story]] Every colored palette on the dial comes in two readings, and the two are a small history of how humanity understood color. Before Newton, color meant PIGMENT: yellow, red and blue were the primaries, and orange, green and purple were mixed from them — the painter's wheel of six, the world of matter. After Newton split a sunbeam with a prism, a second world opened: red, green and blue LIGHT, with cyan, magenta and yellow between them — six hues again, the world of energy. DOMY's two palette styles are exactly these two wheels.

[[On the Dial]] Switching between them keeps every positional meaning and only re-tunes the hues. The arm that means Calm stays at the pre-dawn hour whether it is painted the ancients' deep pigment blue or lit as the prism's pure primary; Sorrow stays at midnight, but its mourning purple becomes magenta — the one color that lives in no rainbow, existing only where the two ends of the spectrum are joined by the eye. The most telling case is the center. In paint, the six arm hues pile together into mud, and the white-gold Sun at the hub is the one shade a painter can never mix — so it stays empty of color, the unmixable source. In light, the same six beams thrown inward SUM back to white, and the Sun is vindicated: it is literally the sum of its own dial. Same week, same meanings, two ways of seeing.

metals.title: Gold, Silver, Bronze
metals.base:
[[On the Dial]] Alchemy gave each of the two great lights a metal — gold to the Sun, silver to the Moon — and the dial keeps the pairing where it belongs. On the star, the top tip may be crowned in gold and the bottom tip in silver: the day metal above at noon, the night metal below at midnight. These are accents of TIME, not colors of any arm — they mark the two poles of the day rather than a mood or a virtue.

[[The Plate]] A third metal, bronze, is a matter of finish rather than symbol. Several of the owner's art sets — the Greek and Norse gods, the guild professions, the ring letters — are drawn on bronze plate, and each can be worn as bronze (the art exactly as struck), as gold, or as silver. The gold and silver looks are computed live: a warm-hue detector finds only the bronze pixels of a medallion and shifts them toward gold or cool silver, leaving the gray stone and engraving untouched, so the plate changes metal without the scene changing. The ring letters follow the same three-metal logic — a gold master and a pre-rendered silver, with bronze a straight warm multiply — and on the ring the triangle of one metal is always set against its counter-metal, so the six-letter Seal reads as a true two-tone hexagram.

ring_letters.title: The Ring Letters
ring_letters.base:
[[On the Dial]] Four letters ride the outer ring of the standard dial and spell the clock's name: D, O, M, Y. The O is written as the Greek Ω (omega), which looks like the reason it is there — but the real reason is hidden in the numbers, and once seen it cannot be unseen. Each of the four is a Greek letter, and each sits at the hour equal to its place in the Greek alphabet. Δ delta is the 4th letter, and it stands at 4h. M mu is the 12th, and it crowns 12h at the top. Y upsilon is the 20th, and it marks 20h in the evening. Ω omega is the 24th and last, and it takes the bottom, 24h, midnight — the end of the alphabet at the end of the day. The brand name and the clock face are the same object: DOMY is four Greek ordinals that each point to their own hour.

[[The Lesson]] That is why omega closes the word and the day together — the last letter guarding the darkest hour — and why the top of the ring is an M: mu, twelfth of twenty-four, exactly halfway, standing where noon stands. The trio of virtues reads straight off these letters, too: Faith points up from the M at 12h, Love closes the word at the Y at 20h, Hope waits at the D at 4h before the dawn.

[[On the Dial]] The feminine variant ring, MORPH, is built on the same secret. Its glyphs are mu (12th) at 12h, pi (16th) at 16h, omega (24th) at 24h and eta (8th) at 8h — every letter again seated at its alphabetical hour, a different word written in the same hidden clock. The number ring is the plain confession of the trick: it simply prints 12, 16, 20, 4 and 8 at those hours and keeps omega at 24, the one hour whose letter has always been its number.

sun.title: Sunday — the Dual Center
sun.base:
[[The Hour]] Sunday does not sit among the days — the days sit around Sunday. Every other weekday takes an arm of the star; the Sun takes the center, white-gold, because it is not one color among six but the sum of all of them. The six arm-hues are its light decomposed, the star is the prism, and Sunday is what goes into it. It rules no single hour because it rules all of them: the Joy of noon, the Passion of evening, the Calm before dawn are one light seen at different angles, and the center is where they all still belong to each other. That is why the Sun keeps no colored arm and no single mood, but the two the sky itself stages — Glory at full noon and Awe when the light fails.

[[The Story]] No pantheon could resist fusing this day's god with kingship, and the page wears four of them. Helios drives the quadriga and sees everything done under the open sky — witnesses swore their oaths by the one nothing escapes. Ra sails the day-barque from horizon to horizon and dies into the Duat each night to be reborn at dawn, sun and king and temple in one body. Amaterasu, from whom Japan's emperors claim descent, shut herself in the cave of Ama-no-Iwato and left the world in the dark until her own reflection in a mirror drew her out — the day called Nichiyōbi, sun-day, its very glyph 日 the one that opens the name of Japan. And Dažbog, the giving sun of the owner's home mythology, pours grain and gold and warmth from the center and keeps nothing back — the Slavs called themselves his grandchildren. Four faiths sit on this day for one reason each: Christianity because Constantine made the day of the Sun the day of rest, and Mithraism underneath it, whose dies Solis crowned the Unconquered Sun through seven planetary grades.

[[On the Dial]] The center's calling is the Ruler — but it is a DOUBLE calling, one figure with two faces, the dial's own yin-yang. As the bright Ruler the Sun practices Justice, the light that falls on everyone alike, and is stalked by Pride, the self at the middle mistaking itself for the source of the light. As the dark Servant it practices Humility, the source remembering it is not the whole, and is stalked by Servility, faithful sunrise gone slack into mere routine. And here the cure that everywhere else crosses the dial stays home: the Servant's Humility cures the Ruler's Pride, the Ruler's Justice straightens the Servant's Servility, an angel and a devil on the same shoulders, each the other's medicine. Its metal is Gold, the incorruptible, laid on as leaf and never mixed — the unmixable center made a goldsmith's fact — crowning the star's top tip at noon. Its element, on the Japanese week, is not one of the five but the sun itself, 日, heralded by Yatagarasu the three-legged crow who rides the solar disc.

[[The Lesson]] So Sunday is the one seat on the wheel with no opposite to answer to, because it is its own opposite: king and servant, glory and eclipse, the disease and the medicine in a single white-gold body. Every other day is cured from across the dial. Sunday is cured from within.

moon.title: Monday — the Physician's Calm
moon.base:
[[The Hour]] Monday belongs to the Moon, and its arm stands at 4h, the hour before the world stirs, painted in the dial's stillest blue. The mood there is Calm — not the calm of nothing happening, but the calm of a night that has already forgiven yesterday and has not yet been asked about tomorrow. The Moon is the fastest of the dial's slow hands, the phlegmatic humor made light: cool, silver, borrowed rather than made, because every scrap of its glow is the Sun's own light handed on across the dark. Where the Sun rules every hour by being the sum of them, the Moon rules exactly one — the hour when it alone is visible.

[[The Story]] The lunar gods are travelers before they are anything else, and the page's faces agree. Selene drives her own team of white horses low across the night, sister to Helios and aunt, in some tellings, to the dawn — the Greek root under this whole set, the moon read as a chariot rather than a face. Máni, the Norse Moon, is chased forever across the sky by the wolf Hati, a hunt that only ends at Ragnarök — the Norse week's answer to why the moon never simply stops to rest. Khonsu, "the traveller" of the Egyptian night, crosses the sky as physician and timekeeper both, his name meaning to cross or traverse — the healer's calm carried into myth centuries before medicine had a calendar. And Hors, of the owner's home Slavic set, is the deity of the night light that the sorcerer-prince Vseslav is said to have crossed paths with before dawn in the Tale of Igor's Campaign — the 4h arm told as chronicle. The day's own faith is Islam, and it could hardly be more literal: the crescent moon opens and closes Ramadan by sighting alone, the Quran names the crescents as mankind's timekeeping (2:189), and Laylat al-Qadr, the Night of Power, falls in the lunar dark of the month's last third. Underneath, Druidism keeps the same clock in groves and tides — a religion that reckoned its festivals by the moon before Rome ever fixed a calendar.

[[On the Dial]] The Moon's calling is the Physician, a choice medieval medicine took seriously: bloodletting calendars were drawn up against the phases, and the healer's real work — the night watch at a sickbed, the hand that stays calm when the patient cannot — is Serenity practiced as a trade. Its shadow is Fear, older than any list of sins, the same unlit dark that, accepted, becomes Serenity, and dreaded becomes the oldest terror there is. The full cure crosses the dial complete: Mars' Courage reaches over the center from the working afternoon to steady what frightens the small hours, while the Moon sends its own Serenity back down the same line to quell Mars' Wrath — the night-watch and the warrior, each the other's medicine. Its metal is Silver, alchemy's Luna: the albedo, the mirror's face, and the only metal whose ordinary tarnish and polish already look like a moon working through its phases. On the Japanese week Monday keeps no elemental disguise at all — Getsuyōbi is simply the Moon's own day, 月, exactly as Sunday keeps the Sun's own 日, the two luminaries standing outside the five-element cycle the other five days share.

[[The Lesson]] So Monday's medicine does not live at home, the way Sunday's does, nor wholly across a single quiet axis — it lives on the far side of the dial's loudest arm, in Mars' fire. Calm and Zeal are opposite moods, blue against orange, but the two bodies that carry them are bound tighter than mood alone: the physician steadies the soldier's nights, and the soldier lends the physician the nerve that pure calm cannot supply on its own.

mars.title: Tuesday — the Soldier's Ember
mars.base:
[[The Hour]] Tuesday belongs to the red planet, and the portrait is honest about the name: Mars is the color of iron oxide and dried blood, a vast dark canyon torn across its equator so the god of war actually looks wounded. Yet its arm at 16h is not red but ember orange, because the arm colors belong to the hours, and 16h is the working afternoon — the mood of Zeal. Orange is fire domesticated: past noon's blaze, far from evening's blood-red, the fuel-fire of effort. Zeal rules the afternoon because that is where work is won or lost — morning's freshness spent, evening's rest not yet earned, only the stubborn ember carrying a task to its end. Mars, the planet of drive, is exactly the body to stoke it.

[[The Story]] The war gods gather thickest on this day, and the page wears the ones who complicate the rage. Ares is battle whole — the Greeks gave him a planet and a weekday while keeping him at arm's length; in the Iliad Zeus calls him the most hateful of the gods, and the courage belongs to the soldier who masters him. Tyr corrects the picture: the one-handed Norse god of war AND justice, who laid his own right hand in the wolf Fenrir's jaws to seal a binding oath — courage in the service of order, the reason the day carries Freemasonry, the Craft sworn on oaths and built with a soldier's discipline. Svetovid, from the owner's Slavic set, keeps four faces and a white divining horse at Arkona, the war-arm's watchman facing every direction at once. And on the Japanese week the day is Kayōbi, fire-day: its planet 火星 Kasei is literally the "fire star," and its medallion crosses two katana under a horned kabuto in a wreath of flame — for fire, like Mars, is birth and war in one, the element that tempers steel and burns the house down with the same heat. Beneath these the alternate faith is Zoroastrianism, whose sacred fire Atar guards asha, the truth that is the courage of the flame against the lie; Montu, Thebes' falcon war god, stands with them unshown.

[[On the Dial]] The calling is the Soldier, and no profession wears its virtue and vice so plainly, because they are the body's own: Courage, the flame that defends, and Wrath, the same flame let loose on its own side. A soldier is precisely that pair walking. Its metal is Iron — the blade, the armor, and the iron that genuinely runs red in the blood, so Mars flows breath by breath in every vein. And its cure hangs straight across the dial at 4h, on Monday's arm: the Moon's Serenity reaches over the center to quell Mars' Wrath, while Mars sends Courage back down the same axis to cure the Moon's Fear. The warrior and the night-watch physician heal each other — day's nerve steadying night's unease, night's calm banking day's fire.

[[The Lesson]] That axis is the whole of Tuesday's place on the wheel. It is an ordinary working day, an arm and not a center, and it knows exactly where its medicine lives: not within, as Sunday's does, but on the far side of the dial, in the still blue hour before dawn. Mars burns; the Moon cools it. Courage and Calm are one line drawn through the center of the day.

mercury.title: Wednesday — the Merchant's Midnight
mercury.base:
[[The Hour]] Wednesday belongs to Mercury, and its arm falls at the very bottom of the dial, 24h, midnight — the one hour with no distractions left. The color there is purple, red mourning dissolved into blue stillness, and the mood is Sorrow: not despair but depth, the mind's own hour, when the day is dead and tomorrow unborn and thought is left to run on alone. Fittingly it is the fastest planet that owns the slowest, stillest hour of the day — Mercury never strays far from the sun in the sky, and on the dial it is given the one moment furthest from any of the sun's own light.

[[The Story]] The messenger gods share one trait across every tradition that draws them: none of them merely deliver, all of them cross a border no one else can. Hermes guides souls to the underworld as psychopomp as readily as he guides merchants to a fair price — invention, theft and diplomacy in the same quick hands, the Greek root of the whole set. Odin pays for the messenger's wisdom rather than being born with it: an eye given at Mimir's well, nine nights hung on Yggdrasil for the runes, wisdom bought at the highest price a god can pay — which is why the day's alternate faith is Shamanism, Odin's ordeal read as the oldest initiation of the walker between worlds. Thoth, scribe of the Egyptian gods and reckoner of years, is close enough to Hermes that the Greeks fused the two outright as Hermes Trismegistus — the elder mirror standing exactly where the myth predicts he should. And Veles, horned lord of cattle, trade, magic, oaths and song, coils as the serpent at the roots of the Slavic world-tree — the owner's home pantheon seating its trickster-sage at precisely this hour, the dial's own midnight root. The day's central faith is Taoism: in the older East Asian week Wednesday is already the water-star day, and water is the Tao's central image, the softest thing that wears down the hardest — Odin's long search for wisdom answering Laozi's quiet one across two very different mythologies.

[[On the Dial]] The calling is the Merchant, Hermes' own trade — the god of roads and marketplaces lending his name to the profession that lives by knowing a thing's true worth. That knowing is Wisdom, the mind that weighs the day honestly at midnight because no one is watching; its shadow is Greed, the same clear sight narrowed until it only counts. The cure runs the dial's full vertical spine: Jupiter's Generosity pours down from the crown at noon to answer Mercury's grasping floor, while Mercury sends Wisdom back up the same line to curb Jupiter's Excess — the open hand and the counting mind correcting each other from opposite ends of one axis. Its metal is Quicksilver, the only one of the seven liquid at room temperature and the tria prima's own spirit — the transformer between states, exactly as Mercury is the planet that transforms a mere fact into an exchange. On the Japanese week Wednesday is Suiyōbi, water-day, 水: the same water that stands for the Tao and for the metal in the same breath, patient, formless, and stronger than anything that tries to stand against it.

[[The Lesson]] So Wednesday's medicine, like Tuesday's, is a straight line rather than a knot: not sideways to a neighboring arm but clean through the center, floor to crown. Sorrow at midnight and Joy at noon are the day's two extremes, and the whole vertical axis of the dial is the argument that a grasping mind and an overfull hand are, in the end, the same lesson taught from two different heights.

jupiter.title: Thursday — the Teacher's Feast
jupiter.base:
[[The Hour]] Thursday belongs to Jupiter, and its arm crowns the very top of the dial at noon, the hour the star's gold tip itself points at. The color is yellow and the mood is Joy — the day's peak, the most light and the most warmth the wheel has to give, and it is no accident of language that "jovial" means, literally, "of Jupiter." Where Mercury owns the dial's stillest, loneliest hour, Jupiter owns its loudest and most generous — the two meeting at opposite ends of the same vertical line, the crown and the floor of one axis.

[[The Story]] Every tradition gives this hour its king, and each king rules by abundance rather than by hoarding. Zeus, the Greek root of the set, rules Olympus from a throne of thunder and, whatever his failings elsewhere in the myths, presides as the sky father whose feast never runs short. Thor answers him in the north, not as king but as protector — Mjölnir defending Asgard and Midgard alike, the same benevolent force turned to guarding rather than ruling, which is why his hammer, not a throne, is the day's Norse emblem. Amun, hidden king of the Egyptian gods, is joined with the sun itself as Amun-Ra — Jupiter's crown worn in Nile dress, kingship and divinity fused into one figure exactly as the noon arm fuses Joy with Glory. And Perun, thunderer of the oak at the dial's own crown in the owner's Slavic set, carries a six-petal thunder-mark, the gromovnik still carved on roof beams against lightning — a folk emblem that is itself a six-armed star, the hexa pointer's own shape read back out of peasant carpentry. The day's ruling faith is Hinduism: Thursday is Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati, who simply IS Jupiter in Vedic astrology, and the week's most devotional day besides. Beneath it Sikhism keeps the same guru's-day literally — a faith built on ten Gurus and practiced in the langar, the free communal kitchen that turns generosity into a daily, physical fact.

[[On the Dial]] The calling is Priest or Teacher — Brihaspati is the gods' own guru, and the jovial bishop of the old woodcuts keeps an open table to match. Generosity is the virtue that follows: faith practiced with the hands, giving before any return is proven. Its shadow is Excess, the same open table with no floor under it, the gluttony of a king who cannot stop giving or taking past the point of sense. The cure runs the dial's vertical spine entire: Mercury's Wisdom rises from midnight to teach the high hand when enough is enough, while Jupiter's own Generosity answers Mercury's Greed from the opposite pole — the two ends of one line correcting each other, crown to floor. Its metal is Tin, the benevolent alloy-maker of the old workshops: bell bronze owes its voice to tin, and a bent bar of it answers back with the famous "tin cry" — a metal that, true to its planet, makes other metals sing rather than keeping the credit for itself. On the Japanese week Thursday is Mokuyōbi, wood-day, 木: the living, growing material, patient and providing, that a carpenter's whole trade is built from — Jupiter's abundance read as timber.

[[The Lesson]] So noon and midnight are bound as tightly as any pair on the dial, though they never touch — Jupiter's crown answering Mercury's floor across the full height of the wheel, Joy and Sorrow, giving and counting, each the correction the other quietly needs.

venus.title: Friday — the Artist's Passion
venus.base:
[[The Hour]] Friday belongs to Venus, and its arm stands at 20h, the evening, painted in the sunset's own blood-red. The mood is Passion, and the dial means it literally: this is the hour the evening star itself opens over the horizon, the point where the day stops demanding and starts desiring — the work of the afternoon let go, and what remains is what a person actually loves. Venus is the reddest hour on the wheel not because the planet itself is red, but because the sky performs its own passion at exactly the hour the goddess claims.

[[The Story]] Love and beauty gather every face they can on this arm, and each tradition adds a complication the last one lacks. Aphrodite, born of sea-foam in the Greek root of the set, is desire without apology — beauty as a force of nature rather than a virtue to be earned. Freya answers her in the north carrying a different weight: goddess of love, yes, but also of war and of gold, riding to claim half the battle's dead in a chariot of cats — passion and ferocity sharing one Norse face, which is exactly why the etymology of Friday itself is disputed between her and Frigg. Hathor, lady of love, beauty, music and joy in the Egyptian set, rules the west and the setting sun in the same office — the evening star's Egyptian twin, tenderness and the horizon in one goddess. And Mokoš, the great spinner and only goddess in Vladimir's Kyiv pantheon, survived Christianization as St Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa — Friday BY NAME, the Slavic evening still carrying her thread. The day's central faith is Christianity, and the owner's framing is direct: after an Old Testament God who punishes, the Christian God forgives everything, a faith whose first commandment is love landing squarely on the day of Love — "God is love" (1 John 4:8), and the story's own center falls on a Friday, the Cross of Good Friday, sacrifice made from love rather than duty. Beneath it Babylon keeps Ishtar, who IS Venus outright — morning star and evening star by turns, love and war in one goddess exactly as the planet itself alternates sky positions, and whose priesthood first named the planets as gods in the order this very week still keeps.

[[On the Dial]] The calling is the Artist — the planet's "children" in the old woodcuts are musicians and lovers, and Love is the virtue that follows: passion given enough patience to become form, the artist's discipline and the lover's both. Its shadow is Jealousy, not love's opposite but love's fear — the dread of losing what is loved, the mirror where comparison first begins. The cure crosses the dial to the morning arm: Saturn's Patience reaches over from 8h to heal Jealousy, insecurity outwaited rather than argued with, while Venus sends Love back the same way to dissolve Saturn's Envy — the two slowest arts on the wheel, waiting and loving, mending each other from opposite sides. Its metal is Copper, aes cyprium, named for Venus' own island of Cyprus; the ♀ glyph is read as her hand-mirror, and polished copper was the first mirror humanity ever owned — vanity and love sharing one bright metal. On the Japanese week Friday is Kinyōbi, metal-day, 金: gold's own character standing in for Venus, the one day of the five-element cycle where the element is a treasure rather than a raw material.

[[The Lesson]] So Friday's medicine lies on the far side of the dial from where its own fire burns, in Saturn's slow green morning — passion needing patience the way a flame needs a lamp, and Saturn's patient waiting needing exactly the warmth Venus never runs short of.

saturn.title: Saturday — the Farmer's Patience
saturn.base:
[[The Hour]] Saturday belongs to Saturn, and its arm falls at 08h, the morning — the paradox the whole wheel is built to display, because it hands the freshest hour to the slowest planet. The color is green and the mood is Renewal: dew and growth on turned soil, and no body on the dial understands that mornings return better than the one that takes thirty years to complete a single circle of its own. It is the week's day of rest that keeps this hour on behalf of all the others, and whatever was buried in the dark of the night before comes up green by 08h.

[[The Story]] Every tradition that draws this day draws something that waits, dies, or both. Cronus, the Greek root of the set, devoured his own children out of fear of a prophecy — the harvest god turned hoarder, the sickle that reaps but also the one that cannot let go. Old Norse gave Saturday no god of its own — laugardagr simply means washing day — so the owner's canon seats Loki here instead: the bound trickster as Cronus' northern mirror, whose envy of the beloved Baldr is the green arm's own vice made myth, and whose sentence, bound until the world's end, is Patience taught the hardest way there is. Osiris, in the Egyptian set, is the harvest that dies and returns, lord of the underworld and of rebirth at once — Saturn's patience made into an entire theology of the sickle. And Morana, the owner's home Slavic goddess of winter and death, is drowned or burned as a straw effigy at the spring equinox to this very day — the arm of Renewal enacted as literal, yearly ritual rather than left as metaphor. The day's central faith is Judaism: Shabbat itself, and the Hebrew name for the planet, Shabtai, is drawn directly from the Sabbath it keeps. Beneath it Voodoo keeps Baron Samedi — "Baron Saturday" by name — keeper of the cemetery crossroads and of the dead, patience read as the vigil kept at the threshold between the living and the gone.

[[On the Dial]] The calling is the Farmer, Cronus' own sickle turned to a gentler use: the one who sows and waits and does not stare too long at the neighbor's field. Patience is that waiting made a virtue; its shadow is Envy, the oldest failure of those who wait — the covetous glance at a richer harvest next door, patience gone sour. The cure crosses to the evening arm: Venus' Love reaches over from 20h to dissolve Envy, warmth loosening the cold grip on what someone else has, while Saturn sends Patience back the same way to heal Venus' Jealousy — insecurity outwaited rather than argued with. Its metal is Lead, heaviest and slowest of the seven, the alchemists' prima materia — the base matter the whole Great Work begins from, so that lead-to-gold reads as the week itself in miniature, Saturday's patient waiting finally paying out as Sunday's gold. On the Japanese week Saturday is Doyōbi, earth-day, 土: the ground itself, the last and heaviest of the five elements, exactly where a planet this slow and this patient belongs.

[[The Lesson]] So Saturday's medicine lies clean across the dial in Venus' evening red — the farmer's waiting needing exactly the warmth the artist never runs short of, and the week closes its circle precisely where it is cured: the slowest arm reaching for the warmest one, patience and love mending each other before the wheel turns again to Sunday.

Justice.base:
[[The Figure]] Justice belongs to Sunday's bright face, the Ruler enthroned at the dial's center in white-gold — the virtue a ruler PRACTICES, not merely wears. It is Solomon dividing the child, the heart weighed against Ma'at's feather, the sun that falls on everyone alike: light and law that do not play favorites. Because Sunday is the double day, this is only half its calling — the crown that rules must also answer for how it rules.

[[The Plate]] Cross the center's own inner axis and the cure runs backward, not out to another day: Justice heals the Servant's Servility, the spine gone slack in service of the throne rather than of what is right — what is right is exactly what a bent spine needs. The medallion sets him on his own throne: a golden scale balanced in one hand, a sword sheathed and kept upright in the other — judgment without cruelty — under a noon halo blazing straight up like the sunburst it sits in.

Humility.base:
[[The Figure]] Humility is Sunday's dark face, the Servant's grace, worn in black with silver trim opposite the Ruler's gold. The one who stands at the middle of everything is the one most tempted to believe the light is his; humility is the source remembering it is not the whole — Amaterasu drawn from her cave by her own reflection is its oldest picture, the sun shown that it is a light among lights.

[[The Plate]] Its cure crosses the center's inner axis to the Ruler's own Pride — the dial's one seat that is both its own disease and its own medicine. The badge answers Justice's throne with a basin: the same king's Servant face, crown lifted from his own head and held low in both hands, one bare foot set in water — the washing of feet is the throne's private mirror.

Serenity.base:
[[The Figure]] Serenity rides Monday's blue, the Moon's calm at the still hour before dawn. It is the settled surface of a mind at peace in the dark, ruling 4h because that is where calm is earned: nothing left of yesterday, nothing yet of tomorrow, only borrowed silver light — the phlegmatic hour of the old humors.

[[The Plate]] Sent across the dial, that stillness is medicine: the night-watch's calm quells the Wrath burning on Tuesday's orange arm, the calm hand laid on the raised one. The emblem holds its whole mood in one closed pair of eyes — a seated figure meditating beside a lake so still the crescent moon mirrors on it without a single ripple.

Courage.base:
[[The Figure]] Courage rides Tuesday's orange, Mars' fire pointed the right way. The same flame that can burn a house down tempers the steel; courage is that fire mastered, the soldier who holds the line rather than the one who loses his temper.

[[The Plate]] It answers the Moon straight across the wheel: the day's nerve reaches over the center to cure the Fear that pools on Monday's blue before dawn, the warrior steadying the frightened. The emblem is a single held line: a shield raised into a gathering storm, a spear planted upright in the ground like a promise kept rather than a threat made.

Wisdom.base:
[[The Figure]] Wisdom rides Wednesday's purple, Mercury's clear sight at midnight — the mind that weighs the day honestly because no one is watching. It is Odin's eye given for a drink at Mimir's well, understanding bought at a price.

[[The Plate]] Its cure travels the dial's whole vertical spine: from the bottom it curbs Thursday's Excess overhead, knowing at the height of the feast when enough is enough. The badge's owl needs no lantern to see by — it holds one aloft anyway, over a book already glowing with its own twilight script, a wisdom that still bothers to light the page for others.

Generosity.base:
[[The Figure]] Generosity rides Thursday's yellow, Jupiter's open hand, the jovial king's table spread for everyone. It is faith practiced with the hands, giving before any return is proven.

[[The Plate]] Its cure pours straight down the dial's spine to the midnight arm: the full hand answers Wednesday's Greed, the giving top curing the grasping floor. The medallion is pure verb — one open hand pouring an endless stream of coin and ripe wheat down into a ring of hands cupped to receive it, noon light behind.

Love.base:
[[The Figure]] Love rides Friday's red, Venus' virtue and the greatest of the three theological virtues — the one word that belongs both to the seven cross-cure pairs and to Faith, Hope and Love. It is passion given patience enough to become form, the artist's work and the lover's.

[[The Plate]] Across the dial it dissolves Saturday's Envy, warmth loosening the cold grip on what the neighbor has. The emblem builds a nest rather than a grip: two hands cupped around one rose in full bloom, doves circling the sunset-red light above it.

Patience.base:
[[The Figure]] Patience rides Saturday's green, Saturn's slow art, learned from a planet that takes thirty years to close one circle. It is the farmer who sows and waits and does not stare too long at the next field.

[[The Plate]] Its cure crosses to the evening arm and heals Friday's Jealousy — insecurity waited out, not argued with, the two slow arts of waiting and loving mending each other. The badge plants two ages at once: a farmer kneeling to sow a single seed beside a slow hourglass, while a sapling from an earlier, already-answered patience stands grown at his side.

Pride.base:
[[The Figure]] Pride is Sunday's Ruler face gone wrong, worn in the same white-gold as Justice — the self at the center of everything, the oldest sin because it is the center's own. The star that gives all light mistakes itself for the light's owner: Phaethon seizing the reins, the king who forgets he serves.

[[The Plate]] Its only cure is the Servant's Humility, and the cure never leaves the center — the middle heals itself or nothing does. The medallion catches him mid-lie: gazing into a gilded mirror that doubles and enlarges his own crown, peacock feathers fanned behind the throne into a second, false halo.

Servility.base:
[[The Figure]] Servility is Sunday's Servant face curdled, black with silver trim like Humility but with the grace gone out of it. Where the Servant's true calling is a spine bent in service of the truth, servility is a spine with no bones at all, serving the throne instead of what is right — faithful sunrise gone slack into mere routine.

[[The Plate]] Its cure is the Ruler's own Justice, reaching crosswise inside the center: what is right straightens what has gone limp. The badge draws the difference from Humility in one stroke — kneeling looks the same, but here thin strings run up from the shoulders into the dark, service become a puppet's.

Fear.base:
[[The Figure]] Fear is Monday's blue turned cold, the Moon's dark face and older than any list of sins. It is the same unlit night that, once accepted, is Serenity; dreaded instead, it is the oldest terror, the frightened mind waking in the small hours.

[[The Plate]] Its full cure stands straight across the dial: Tuesday's Courage reaches over the center to steady it. The emblem's real monster is a shadow — the same cold moon that grants Serenity its stillness here stretches a small hunched figure's own outline into something to flee from.

Wrath.base:
[[The Figure]] Wrath is Tuesday's orange loosed on its own side, Mars' fire without a hand on the reins — the shout on the war god's face, the lion on his shield read as ruin instead of defense.

[[The Plate]] Its cure comes from the pre-dawn arm: Monday's Serenity, the calm that banks a furnace before it spills. The logo shows the Soldier's own gear turned against itself — shield shattered, spear broken, only a bare fist wreathed in flame left standing where Courage's shield used to be raised.

Greed.base:
[[The Figure]] Greed is Wednesday's purple gone grasping, Mercury's shadow — the merchant's cleverness turned to mere counting, the same mind that understands the world begins only to tally it.

[[The Plate]] Its cure falls the whole height of the dial: Thursday's Generosity, the open hand answering the closed one. The badge's joke is exact — a padlock still snapped shut on a lid already open, coin spilling uncounted through fingers too full to close around anything.

Excess.base:
[[The Figure]] Excess is Thursday's yellow overflowing, Jupiter's vice — the royal table that never stops, the gluttony of a king who cannot stop giving or taking past the point of sense. It is generosity with no floor.

[[The Plate]] Its cure rises from the opposite pole: Wednesday's Wisdom, the deep mind teaching the high hand when to stop reaching. The emblem's table has already lost the argument with itself, collapsing under its own plenty while the guest it was meant to feed slumps back exhausted.

Jealousy.base:
[[The Figure]] Jealousy is Friday's red curdled, Venus' shadow — not love's opposite but love's fear, the dread of losing what is loved, the mirror where comparison begins. Love that has started to grip what it should only warm.

[[The Plate]] Its cure crosses from the morning arm: Saturday's Patience, insecurity outwaited rather than argued. The badge's rose is the same one Love cups gently — here a serpent coils around it possessively, and the flower is already wilting in the grip meant to keep it.

Envy.base:
[[The Figure]] Envy is Saturday's green soured, Saturn's bitter shadow and the oldest failure of those who wait — the covetous glance at the neighbor's richer field, Loki's grudge against Baldr. It is patience gone sour.

[[The Plate]] Its cure reaches from the evening: Friday's Love, warmth dissolving the cold arithmetic of what someone else has. The emblem's wall is deliberately low, easy to see over, impossible to cross with dignity — a hand reaching through the fence toward wheat that stays, on principle, just out of grasp.

The Wheel of Moods.base:
[[The Story]] People have always tried to draw a map of their own feelings, and every age drew it with the tools it trusted. The Greeks drew it with medicine: four bodily fluids — blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile — made four temperaments, the sanguine, the phlegmatic, the choleric and the melancholic. It was a map of PERSONS: you were one of the four, more or less for life.

Modern psychology drew it as a wheel. Robert Plutchik arranged eight primary emotions in a color circle — joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger, anticipation — opposites facing each other across the hub, neighbors blending into compound feelings: joy and trust make love, fear and surprise make awe. It is a map of INSTINCTS, the reactions every animal carries.

[[The Hour]] The dial draws the third map, and its trick is time. Its eight are not four kinds of people and not eight reflexes — they are the HOURS of one day, temperaments every person passes through between two midnights: Joy at noon, Zeal in the working afternoon, Passion in the evening, Sorrow at midnight, Calm before dawn, Renewal in the morning — with Glory, the white sum of every color, seated in the center, and Awe, the servant's mood, watching it from below.

[[The Lesson]] Set the three maps side by side and they agree more than they differ. Joy sits in both wheels, and in both it is yellow; Zeal matches Plutchik's anticipation, orange in both; Sorrow is his sadness; the morning green that the dial calls Renewal is the green he calls trust — the morning is the day trusting that the light returns. And Awe is not even a translation: fear plus surprise is precisely Plutchik's own compound of that name, the feeling of standing under something larger than yourself. The wheel plate reads all of it at a glance: six hours around the rim, the white center, and the small crescent waiting underneath.

Glory.base:
[[The Figure]] Glory is the bright mood of Sunday's Ruler face, white-gold, the sun at its full height — the day given more than it asked for, the king's light at its widest. It is not one hour but the sum of all of them seen at their best.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Awe, the Servant face's dark twin sharing the same center. The medallion's proof is the missing shadow — a crowned figure at the sun's exact zenith, arms thrown open, casting none at all.

Calm.base:
[[The Hour]] Calm rides Monday's blue at 4h, the still and moonlit hour before dawn, the temperature of a world asleep. It rules that hour because that is where calm is earned — nothing left of yesterday, nothing yet of tomorrow, only borrowed silver light.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Zeal, twelve hours around the dial on Tuesday's orange at 16h. The emblem holds its whole mood in one standing leg — a heron motionless at the blue hour, the crescent moon and the morning's first star mirrored on water that has not yet decided to move.

Zeal.base:
[[The Hour]] Zeal rides Tuesday's orange at 16h, the ember of the working afternoon — not noon's blaze and not evening's blood-red but the fuel-fire of effort, the second wind. It rules that hour because that is when a task is won or lost, morning's freshness spent and evening's rest not yet earned.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Calm, Monday's still 4h. The badge stacks two kinds of exertion in one frame — a hammer striking sparks off molten iron and a runner mid-stride trailing embers past the anvil, forge-work and foot-work sharing the same fire.

Sorrow.base:
[[The Hour]] Sorrow rides Wednesday's purple at midnight, red mourning dissolved in blue stillness. Not despair but depth — the mind's hour, when the day is dead and tomorrow unborn and thought runs on alone, kept by the god who guided souls across the last border.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Joy, noon on Thursday's yellow. The emblem's single tear is amethyst, not water — a bowed figure under a midnight sky letting one fall toward water that catches the last purple light before it goes still again.

Joy.base:
[[The Hour]] Joy rides Thursday's yellow at noon, the day's own generosity — the most light, the most warmth, the peak. "Jovial" means, literally, "of Jupiter," and the top of this dial is where the star points at true solar noon.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Sorrow, midnight on Wednesday's purple. The badge's light does not merely shine, it scatters — a figure laughing arms wide beneath the noon sun, bursts of gold breaking off it like fireworks that never quite land.

Passion.base:
[[The Hour]] Passion rides Friday's red at 20h, the sky turned blood-red as the evening star opens over the sunset. It rules that hour because evening is when the day stops demanding and starts desiring — the work lets go and what remains is what one actually loves.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Renewal, 8h on Saturday's green. The emblem's hands never actually meet — two dancers mid-turn in flame-red silk, reaching toward each other across a sky already bleeding the evening's own red.

Renewal.base:
[[The Hour]] Renewal rides Saturday's green at 8h, the morning, dew and growth on turned soil. The paradox of the wheel — the slowest planet given the freshest hour — is its whole point: no body knows better than Saturn that mornings return.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Passion, 20h on Friday's red. The badge needs only one small green fact to make its case — a single sprout breaking dew-soaked soil while sunrise gilds the freshly plowed field it grew from.

The Ninth Mood.base:
[[The Figure]] Eight moods share the day between them, hour by hour — but the dial knows one more, a ninth that belongs to no hour at all. The eight visit every single day, as reliably as noon and midnight; the ninth visits the way a real eclipse does: rarely, briefly, and everything stops to look at it.

[[The Sky]] Eclipse is the EVENT mood — the moment the Servant crosses in front of the Ruler and the two faces of Sunday stand, for one breath, on the same point. Glory is veiled; Awe looks up; and for that instant the scale of the two triangles touches the zero no one can live on. It is not a state a person stays in — it is an instant that passes THROUGH a person, and is gone before the light returns.

[[The Ninth]] The arithmetic is the owner's own. The Nine Intelligences read nine as six Prism arms plus three Trinity arms; the moods read it as EIGHT PLUS ONE — the eight hours of feeling, plus the one moment that stands outside the hours, the way the Compass holds eight arms around one center. Its plate says exactly that: the darkened sun with its thin silver corona, the crown resting on an empty throne, a single candle keeping the room — the wheel's whole light switched off for a moment, on purpose.

[[The Lesson]] And that is why the ninth exists. When the eclipse passes, Glory does not merely return — it returns SEEN, because everything that shines is measured by the moment it went dark. The eight moods light the day; the ninth is there so the other eight remember they are lit.

Awe.base:
[[The Figure]] Awe is Sunday's Servant face — the mood of standing under something larger than yourself, worn in black with a thin silver trim rather than a color of its own. It is the upturned face at the bottom of the plate: the servant kneeling low, eyes wide, watching the ringed sun blaze at the zenith — fear and wonder held in a single look.

[[The Plate]] Its opposite is Glory, and the two need each other: Glory is being seen, Awe is seeing. Psychology keeps the same recipe — fear plus surprise compound into awe — and the dial keeps the same seat: whoever holds the center is watched from below, and whoever watches from below keeps the center honest.

Spring.title: Spring
Spring.base:
[[The Sky]] Spring is the arc between two crossings: the equinox that opens it, when the sun's path along the ecliptic touches the celestial equator and its declination falls to zero, and the solstice that closes it, when the sun's climb north peaks at +23.44 degrees — the same tilt-born number that draws the Tropic of Cancer and, elsewhere on this dial, decides who gets a Spring at all rather than a Wet season. Day and night are only approximately equal ON the equinox itself; the sun's own disc and the air's bending of its light lift sunrise a few minutes early and hold sunset a few minutes late, so the true equal split — the equilux — lands a few days to either side, a small discrepancy the dial's own standards would rather name than smooth over.

[[On the Dial]] On the Seasons pointer Spring wears green — and green here is not a compromise between yellow and blue but Goethe's own third color, the exact union of his two poles in equal parts, precisely what an equinox itself performs with daylight and dark. Geometrically the season occupies the quarter arc that opens at the 06h position — the same clock position that carries the octa pointer's dawn arm on the day below it — and climbs clockwise to the top, where the summer solstice arm stands; the wheel stretches that unequal real span into an honest 90 degrees, so the corner at 06h still lands on the true equinoctial instant, read off the bundled ephemeris rather than assumed.

[[The Plate]] The green carries an echo rather than an identity: Saturday's own green, worn for an entirely different reason (Saturn's morning Renewal), still lands on the same word — growth resuming after a fallow stretch — and the equal light of the equinox is itself a kind of small justice, day and night weighed out with nothing tipping the scale. Field ethology keeps the same appointment: migratory birds time their return north to this very crossing, reading day-length rather than temperature, the oldest clock there is. The badge reads exactly this balance: a green shoot breaking the soil at the moment of sunrise, and beside it a pair of scales holding dead level — one pan lit, one pan shaded, neither side winning.

Summer.title: Summer
Summer.base:
[[The Sky]] Summer opens where Spring closes: at the solstice, the instant the sun's northward climb along the ecliptic stops and reverses, its declination pinned at the year's maximum, +23.44 degrees — directly overhead, at that moment, for any observer standing on the Tropic of Cancer. It is the longest day and shortest night the northern sky offers, not because the sun moves faster or slower but because the tilted axis has swung this hemisphere as far into the light as the year allows; the season runs on to the next crossing, the autumn equinox, when the sun's arc returns to level ground.

[[On the Dial]] Goethe called yellow the first color of light itself — daylight seen through the thinnest possible veil of dark — and no season wears it more honestly than the one holding the year's brightest instant. On the Seasons pointer Summer is the quarter running clockwise from the top arm, planted on the solstice, to the 18h position, where the autumn equinox stands and the octa pointer's own dusk arm keeps its post on the day below; the wheel again forces the true unequal span into a level 90 degrees, so the corner still falls on the instant itself rather than a convenient date.

[[The Plate]] The hue doubles as noon's own color a level down — Jupiter's yellow, the day's Joy, Generosity its virtue — so Summer's gold is, twice over, the color of abundance given rather than hoarded, glory at its most literal. The badge shows it plainly: a wheat field blazing under a sun standing dead at zenith, a reaper's basket already overflowing, and — the one deliberate absence in the whole image — no shadow cast anywhere, because at the solstice's own noon, at the Tropic itself, there is almost none to cast.

Autumn.title: Autumn
Autumn.base:
[[The Sky]] Autumn runs from the equinox where Summer's light finally levels out to the solstice where it bottoms. Between those two crossings the sun's declination falls from zero back through its full negative range to -23.44 degrees, the mirror descent of Spring's climb — and everywhere outside the tropics the nights visibly overtake the days, the one season named for the very verb it performs.

[[On the Dial]] Where green is the union of Goethe's two poles, red-orange is his Steigerung — yellow intensified by being forced through gathering darkness rather than simply mixed with it, light that does not fade quietly but flares on its way down. On the Seasons pointer Autumn is the quarter from the 18h position — the equinox arm sharing its clock-slot with the octa's own dusk red-orange — clockwise down to the bottom, where the winter solstice stands; the same 90-degree stretch keeps the corners true to the ephemeris even as the real season runs a few days short or long of its neighbors.

[[The Plate]] The color borrows its urgency from the dial's own fire arms — Mars' orange Zeal, Venus' red Passion — without belonging to either; Autumn's red-orange is a third kind of heat, spent rather than raised, the fire of things ending well. Deciduous trees perform the same chemistry the color theory describes: chlorophyll's green withdraws first and uncovers the red and orange pigments that were underneath it all along, an intensification the leaf was hiding, not making. The badge is that fire made literal: a bonfire of fallen leaves burning red-orange against a low dusk sky, more leaves still spiraling down out of bare branches to feed it — light, going down, still fighting.

Winter.title: Winter
Winter.base:
[[The Sky]] Winter opens at its own solstice, the sun's declination bottomed at -23.44 degrees — directly overhead, that instant, for the Tropic of Capricorn alone — and closes at the spring equinox, where the sun's climb crosses back to level. It is the shortest day and longest night the northern sky offers, the same tilt that gilds a Capricorn noon over Tromso's horizon barely at all, if it clears it.

[[On the Dial]] Goethe's blue is the mirror of his yellow: the first color of darkness, dark seen through the thinnest possible veil of light rather than light seen through a veil of dark — which is exactly the low, filtered, blue-toned daylight a winter noon actually gives, even at its brightest. On the Seasons pointer Winter is the quarter from the bottom arm, planted on the solstice, clockwise round to the 06h position, where the spring equinox and the octa's own dawn cyan share a clock-slot; the same forced 90 degrees holds the corners to the true instant.

[[The Plate]] The blue reaches sideways to the dial's own Servant face rather than its blue-hour cousin at 04h: this is the season of Awe, the hour — stretched now to a quarter of the year — when even the Sun's own light fails, and its cure, like the center's, is not fought but waited out. Many northern animals answer with the oldest strategy available to them, sleep: hibernation is winter read as a survival budget, metabolism banked down until the light returns to spend again. The badge holds the stillness whole: one bare tree standing alone in deep snow under a moonlit midnight-blue sky, icicles on its lowest branch catching what cold light there is.

Wet_Season.title: Wet Season
Wet_Season.base:
[[The Sky]] Inside the tropics — the belt within 23.44 degrees of the equator, Tropic of Cancer to Tropic of Capricorn — the sun never leans far enough away to cut a real winter, so the year is felt through rainfall instead of temperature: the sun's high point still migrates north and south with the seasons, dragging the equatorial rain belt (the Inter-Tropical Convergence Zone) back and forth beneath it, and a hemisphere's Wet season is simply the stretch when that belt of storms sits over it.

[[On the Dial]] The Wet season is bounded by equinoxes rather than solstices — the two crossings where the sun's declination passes through zero — and its center of gravity is still the solstice itself, the hemisphere's own high-sun instant: north of the equator the wet half runs March equinox to September equinox, centered on the June solstice at the wheel's top; south of it the same logic runs the other way, September to March, centered on the December solstice at the bottom. The Seasons pointer's cross arms mark the equinox edges and the solstice heart; its octa diagonals, set halfway between them, split the half again into a first act and a second, so a glance places an observer not only in the Wet season but in its rising or its retreat.

[[The Plate]] The badge reads the belt's own weather rather than a calendar page: monsoon rain falling in silver sheets over a lush green jungle canopy, a river already swollen catching the rain's own light, broad leaves glistening under water they cannot shed fast enough.

Dry_Season.title: Dry Season
Dry_Season.base:
[[The Sky]] The Dry season is the Wet season's own other half — same tropical belt, same equinox-bounded reckoning, the difference lying entirely in which hemisphere's high sun is pulling the rain belt away rather than toward it. Where the sun stands overhead its warmth draws the ITCZ's storms with it; the hemisphere it has left behind gets the same six months as clear sky and falling water tables instead.

[[On the Dial]] North of the equator the Dry season runs September equinox to March equinox, centered on the December solstice at the wheel's bottom; south of it, March to September, centered on the June solstice at the top — the exact mirror of the Wet season's own bounds, both halves sharing the same 270/90-degree equinox gates the temperate Spring and Autumn open from. The octa diagonals do here what they do for the Wet season: quarter the half again into a first stretch and a second, so the hover can tell a visitor not just which season but how far into it the year has gotten.

[[The Plate]] The badge trades the Wet season's silver for gold and brown: sun-cracked red earth running out to a shimmering heat-haze horizon, grass bleached to pale gold, a small dust devil spinning up in the distance past a lone baobab's silhouette — the same land, waiting out its half of the year.

Summer_Solstice.title: Summer Solstice
Summer_Solstice.base:
[[The Sky]] The summer solstice is the instant the sun's apparent path along the ecliptic reaches its northernmost point, ecliptic longitude 90 degrees, and its declination peaks at +23.44 degrees — Earth's own axial tilt, no more and no less. At that moment the sun stands directly overhead at local noon for every observer on the Tropic of Cancer, and everywhere north of the equator the day is as long, and the night as short, as the year allows.

[[On the Dial]] The year wheel places this instant exactly at the top — not approximated to the nearest day but read off the bundled ephemeris and pinned to the true crossing, the same standard the dial holds its solar-noon hexagram to every single day. On Sundays the Seasons pointer lights this arm together with its opposite number at the bottom, the year's two solstices glowing at once — the double center's own year-long echo, the Ruler's noon and the Servant's midnight meeting a full revolution apart. The badge borrows the shape of that daily hexagram for the year's own noon: a golden six-rayed star crossing behind a sun pinned motionless at its highest point, a gnomon below it casting a shadow reduced, at true solar noon on the Tropic itself, to almost nothing at all.

Winter_Solstice.title: Winter Solstice
Winter_Solstice.base:
[[The Sky]] The winter solstice mirrors its summer twin exactly: the sun's ecliptic longitude reaches 270 degrees, its declination bottoms out at -23.44 degrees, and it stands directly overhead at local noon only for observers on the Tropic of Capricorn. North of the equator this is the shortest day and longest night the year offers — at high enough latitude, as at Tromso, a noon the sun barely bothers to clear.

[[On the Dial]] The wheel plants this instant at the bottom, exactly opposite its summer counterpart and held to the same ephemeris-true standard rather than a rounded date. It shares the Sunday privilege too: when the Seasons pointer lights both solstice arms together, this is the dark half of that pair, the Servant's own hour stretched from a nightly eclipse to a yearly one. The badge answers gold with silver: a sun barely clearing a snow-bound horizon at its lowest arc of the year, a pale six-rayed hexagram behind it, and a gnomon below casting the longest shadow the year ever asks of it.

Equinox.title: Equinox
Equinox.base:
[[The Sky]] Twice a year the sun's path along the ecliptic crosses the celestial equator and its declination touches exactly zero — around March and September, the two moments this one badge stands for equally, since 'the day of perfect balance' reads the same whichever crossing brought it. Day and night are only approximately equal on the date itself; the sun's disc and the air's own bending of light lift sunrise early and hold sunset late by a few minutes each, so the truly equal split, the equilux, falls a few days to one side — a discrepancy small enough to ignore on the wrist and large enough that this dial's own standards insist on naming it anyway.

[[On the Dial]] The wheel plants the two equinoxes at left and right, at the 06h and 18h clock positions — the very slots the octa pointer already gives to dawn and dusk on the day below — each held to its true crossing rather than a calendar convenience. Where Spring wears Goethe's green union of yellow and blue, the Turning Points badge renders the same balance in metal rather than pigment: the badge splits exactly in half, polished gold meeting oxidized silver at two opposite seams, a gnomon standing dead center and casting a shadow precisely as long as it is tall, one half of the disc lit in full daylight and the other in full silver-blue night — noon's metal and midnight's metal, in perfect equilibrium, for the one day a year they actually are.

Meteorological.title: Meteorological Seasons
Meteorological.base:
[[The Story]] Meteorologists keep a second, plainer calendar alongside the astronomer's: rather than starting each season on a solstice or equinox that drifts by a day or so year to year, they simply hand each season three whole calendar months — December through February, March through May, and so on in the northern reckoning — so that every season is exactly the same length every year and lines up cleanly with the monthly records a weather station has always kept. The trade is honest: the sky's own crossings are abandoned for a ruler-straight quarter of the calendar, in exchange for statistics that never need footnoting.

[[On the Dial]] DOMY doesn't reach for the calendar page, but it does gesture at the same idea: the cross pointer's hover for each meteorological season doesn't start at its solstice or equinox, it CENTERS on it — the halfway point back to the previous cardinal instant and the halfway point forward to the next, so 'Heart:' marks the true astronomical turning point sitting in the middle of a span that runs close to, but is never quite, a tidy three-month block. It is the dial's own way of admitting that the weather lags the sky by about six weeks without pretending the lag is exactly a season wide.

[[The Plate]] The badge marks this distinction rather than hiding it: every Meteorological twin keeps its astronomical Season's own scene — the same growth-ring field, the same copper border, the same wheat or bonfire or bare tree at its center — and adds only one small device, pinned like a wax seal at the lower edge: three calendar leaves bound with twine and a slender thermometer laid across them, the measuring instruments of the human calendar resting quietly on top of the celestial one.

Lucifer.base:
[[The Story]] Lucifer is the morning star — in the old readings the most beautiful of the lights, the bearer of the dawn — who looked at his own shining and ruled it should be worshiped. 'I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the Most High': the one recorded ambition is not to destroy the throne but to sit on it. He is the far end of love of self — pride grown past every correction — and his fall is its oldest measure: like lightning, from heaven, downward.

[[On the Dial]] His sign is the rising triangle — the Flame the ring already carries: fire climbing to a point, aimed at the top of the dial where the crown of noon sits. His color is red, the pigment world's peak — the last intensification light passes through before it turns on itself. On the dial's own Sunday the ruler face carries exactly his vice: Pride, the crown forgetting it is worn on behalf of the whole week.

[[The Sky]] The sky offers a precise image of him. A star can die in two directions, and his is the explosion: the supernova — the star that burns everything it has in one climb, briefly outshining every other light in the sky, exactly as it destroys itself. That is pride's whole arc drawn in physics: the blaze upward, aimed at the top of the dial where noon sits, magnificent for a moment and gone.

[[The Lesson]] On the scale of self he stands at the right-hand extreme — plus infinity, red. What he never had was his antidote: the falling triangle across the beam. Nothing weighed on the other pan, and a scale with one loaded pan is not a scale; it is a fall already in progress.

Judas.base:
[[The Story]] Judas Iscariot kept the purse of the twelve and sold the one he followed for thirty pieces of silver, marking him with a kiss so the torches would take the right man. But the act that sets him at the extreme is not the betrayal — it is the verdict he passed afterward, on himself. He judged Judas unforgivable, and carried out the sentence with his own hands. He is the far end of hatred of self: the servant who rules that he is worth nothing.

[[On the Dial]] His sign is the falling triangle — the Chalice: water pouring to a point, aimed at the dial's midnight. His color is blue, the first color darkness makes, the hue of retreat and of cold. And the sky's image of him is the star's OTHER death: the collapse inward — the star that falls into itself until not even its own light is allowed to leave. The black hole sits where his triangle points, at the bottom of the 24-hour dial: midnight, the swallowed light. On the dial's Sunday the servant face carries exactly his vice: service curdled into self-erasure.

[[The Story]] The same night measures him against his own antidote. Peter failed too — three denials before the rooster crowed — and wept, and came back, and was handed the keys. The difference between the two failures is one decision: Peter let himself be forgiven; Judas ruled that mercy could not reach his case. That ruling is pride's mirror image — deciding your own verdict outranks everyone else's, including the one you betrayed. And the pattern is older than him: Ahithophel, David's wisest counselor, betrayed his king and hanged himself when the plot soured.

[[The Lesson]] On the scale of self he stands at the left-hand extreme — minus infinity, blue. His antidote existed — it had washed his feet that same evening — and he never took it. The falling triangle, alone, falls.

The Union.base:
[[The Figure]] Every person lives somewhere on the beam between the two triangles — loving themselves enough to stand and be defended, doubting themselves enough to be corrected. The two names mark the ends no one should reach, and the fight they define is not a fight between two men: it runs inside one. The man who at once loves and hates himself reads himself from both ends — the love keeps him from being trampled, the hate keeps him honest — and between the two readings he walks upright.

The zero of that scale is reached by no individual: the extremes themselves prove it, two weights that dropped off the pendulum because each swung with nothing on the other side. They never had each other — Lucifer with no falling triangle to doubt him, Judas with no rising one to defend him — so each is the other's missing antidote. Together they would see everything, from both absolute ends at once; and that seat belongs to no one alone.

[[On the Dial]] The dial answers with geometry. Lay the falling triangle over the rising one and the instrument's own star appears — the hexagram: Flame over Chalice, red over blue, the Seal the ring's letters already wear. That is why Sunday, alone of the days, has two faces: a ruler who must not become Lucifer and a servant who must not become Judas, sharing one seat so that neither pole is ever left alone with itself. And between the star's two deaths — the explosion upward and the collapse inward — stands the star that does neither: the Sun, which simply gives its light away, steadily, every day. The white center — Glory, the sum of every color — sits exactly where the two triangles cross.

[[The Lesson]] And if the scale needs a figure standing at its zero, he is not at Judas's end. The one who allowed his own death did not do it out of hatred of self: he gave himself away while never once denying who he was — king and servant in one person, washing feet with the same hands that carry the scepter. Permitting your own execution out of love is the gift of a whole self, not the disposal of a worthless one; that is the zero embodied, the union the two extremes never made. The minus end keeps its own name: the man who decided mercy could not reach him.

Sigma.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven ranked wolves plus Sunday's Alpha-Omega pair fill the Wolf Pack's eight regular seats, one name for what a pack needs at each life stage, all the way down to the servant Omega the ring's own midnight letter already spells. Sigma is the ninth, and unlike every other ninth in this encyclopedia it never came from the science it borrows its name from. The whole alpha-to-omega ladder was drawn up from wolves crowded into captivity in the 1940s; L. David Mech, the researcher who coined "alpha," spent decades afterward trying to retract the term once he learned that real wild packs are just families, no ladder anyone climbs. "Sigma" was invented online long after, for a wolf that opts out of ranking entirely — ethology's ghost, not its opposite pole.

[[The Plate]] The plate shows a wolf mid-departure, and the sculptor has stopped working on it exactly there — head and shoulders finished bronze, hindquarters dissolving into raw uncut stone, walking away into rolling mist with no pack in sight. The rock outcrop where an alpha would stand waits empty in the background. The paw-print trail that circles every other medallion in the set runs out along the bottom quarter and fades to bare stone, and the four roundels that would carry a rank's glyph are left polished and blank — the only plate in the set willing to admit it is unfinished.

No duality is asked of Sigma here, and rightly so: the owner's own note is that it stands equal in stature to the Alpha rather than opposite it, a wolf outside the hierarchy rather than its counterweight. Forcing it onto either end of the Lucifer-Judas scale would invent an opposition the wolf itself never claimed.

[[The Lesson]] The eight ranks below it all answer to a mate, a den, a pup, a pace — real needs of a real pack. Sigma is the seat that reminds the other eight that not every name carved onto this dial was found in the wild; some were only ever a story a species told about itself.

The Swarm.base:
